comments:
This manuscript describes Qsmodels, an experimental integration of Answer Set Programming (ASP) — via lparse/smodels — into the control loop of a Quake 3 Arena bot. The authors present a two-layer architecture (high-level ASP planner + low-level reactive/execution layer), introduce pre-emption rules to handle emergencies without full replanning, and report implementation details and some timing observations (plan extraction can take 6–7s). The paper demonstrates an interesting proof-of-concept: connecting a declarative planner to an interactive, real-time gaming environment and reusing existing low-level game functionality.

Strengths:
- Clear high-level idea: use ASP for mid-/long-term planning while keeping reactive behavior in a low-level loop.
- Practical integration work: grounding and solving are invoked externally, and the system handles sensing, plan extraction and execution.
- The pre-emption rules concept is a sensible mechanism to reduce costly replanning in dynamic domains.

Weaknesses / Issues:
- Evaluation is very limited and largely qualitative. Apart from a single timing figure (6–7s), there is no systematic experimental comparison against standard FSM-based bots or other planners, no metrics (win rates, latency, plan lengths), and no ablation studies (with/without pre-emption rules).
- Important implementation and representational details are missing: encoding of actions/fluents, plan length choices, frequency and content of sensing updates, specifics of the ‘‘simple learning system’’. These are crucial to reproduce or assess the approach.
- The paper notes heavy computational demand but does not quantify how that impacts gameplay across different machines, maps, or numbers/types of opponents.
- Writing and presentation need polishing: the text is repetitive in places, contains formatting glitches and some unclear passages.

Recommendations to authors:
- Add quantitative experiments comparing the ASP-based bot to baseline Q3A bots (e.g., built-in FSM bots) across multiple maps and scenarios, report win rates, reaction latencies, planner runtimes, and plan success rates.
- Provide more details of the ASP encodings, examples of pre-emption rules, and the learning component.
- Discuss limits of the current approach and strategies to reduce solver latency (incremental solving, tighter grounding, switching to API, partial planning, heuristics).
- Clean up the presentation and remove duplicated paragraphs.

Overall, the work is a worthwhile prototype showing feasibility of ASP in an interactive setting but falls short on rigorous evaluation and reproducibility detail. The paper would benefit from more experiments and clearer presentation.
